The session features the coaching work of Magalie Pasha who's my wife, coaching partner and a co-founder of Robin's Bananas Training. So now at this point we're about 30 minutes into the conversation and if you look back you'll see a pattern of Rebecca sharing a self-criticism or a self-judgment and Magalie giving her what we call a moderating reframe. So when people are self-critical or self-judgmental, they're making themselves wrong. And in order to make yourself wrong, you have to be putting yourself in what we call a right-wrong framework. So a right-wrong framework says, hey, everything's either right or wrong. This is how things should be, but that's not how things are. Therefore, things are wrong. And when people feel like things are wrong, they feel frustrated, helpless, and upset. So Magalie responds by providing Rebecca with a moderating reframe. A moderating reframe shows the person that there's more than just right and wrong. And it shows you, you know, it's not just black and white, there's some gray. There's some colors. Right? So an example of a moderating reframe is it's not that he's right and you're wrong, you just have different styles. And it's a good thing that two of you are different in fact because it allows you to have the passion that you have. So that's one moderating reframe. It gives the person more ways to think about their situation. So you're going to hear this happening several times now when the client, when Rebecca brings up a self-judgment or a criticism in terms of right and wrong, Magalie offers a moderating reframe as an alternative. So every time Magalie offers a moderating reframe, she's helping Rebecca to find another way to move forward. Couples need to be different from each other. That's why you have chemistry and polarity.
